title: The Land Between Two Rivers
summary: Southern Iraq has almost nothing — no stone, no metal, no timber, no decent rainfall. What it has is silt, dropped every spring by the Tigris and Euphrates as they flood out of the Anatolian mountains, and the people who learned to control that water didn't get civilized because they were brilliant. They got brilliant because the land forced them to invent government, law, writing, mathematics, and the city or starve. Mesopotamia is not the story of one people but of one place, through which Sumerians, Akkadians, Amorites, Kassites, Assyrians, and Chaldeans passed in a 3,000-year parade.

title: Before the Cities
summary: Around 5000 BCE, villagers at a place called Eridu built a small mud-brick platform and put a temple on top of it — and then for the next three thousand years, every time the temple wore out, they tore it down and rebuilt it on exactly the same spot. Archaeologists found seventeen temples stacked on top of each other. Meanwhile a goddess named Inanna was rising at Uruk, the first irrigation canals were being dug across the southern plain, and blue lapis lazuli from Afghan mountains 3,000 kilometers away was already turning up in southern graves. The villages were small, but they were already wired into a world that stretched from the Indian Ocean to the Mediterranean.

title: The First City — Uruk and the Sumerian Breakthrough
summary: Around 3800 BCE, a settlement called Uruk on a branch of the Euphrates began to grow faster than any human community had ever grown before, and within six hundred years it had 40,000 to 80,000 residents, a monumental White Temple, the wheel, cylinder seals, beer, the world's first writing (cuneiform), and a base-60 number system that still runs your clock and your compass today. The name 'Uruk' may even be the source of the modern word 'Iraq.' This is where the city was invented as a category of human community — and where most of the inventions we associate with civilization were stacked on top of each other inside a single generation.

title: The Age of City-States
summary: By 2900 BCE more than a dozen Sumerian cities were locked in constant low-grade war with each other, mostly over water rights and border farmland. The grudge match between Lagash and Umma produced what may be the world's first historical document — the Stele of the Vultures, which shows the phalanx infantry formation in use 1,500 years before the Greeks supposedly invented it. At Ur, the British archaeologist Leonard Woolley found royal tombs containing 74 attendants who had died alongside their queen, with golden harps and lapis headdresses suggesting a society far richer and more hierarchical than anyone had expected from this era. Gilgamesh, the legendary king of Uruk, lived somewhere in this world.

title: The First Empire — Sargon and Akkad
summary: Around 2334 BCE a man named Sargon — whose name literally means 'The King Is Legitimate,' a name historians have always found suspicious — seized the throne of Kish and built the first multi-ethnic empire in human history, stretching from the Persian Gulf to the Mediterranean. His daughter Enheduanna became the first named author in recorded history, predating every other known writer by more than a century. His grandson Naram-Sin declared himself a god during his own lifetime, a theological move that horrified traditional priests. The whole empire collapsed within a few generations under a combination of Gutian raiders and a century-long drought now known as the 4.2 kiloyear event.

title: The Sumerian Renaissance
summary: Around 2112 BCE the governor of Ur, Ur-Nammu, drove the Gutians out and founded what's now called the Third Dynasty of Ur — a century-long final flowering of Sumerian language, literature, and bureaucracy. Ur-Nammu wrote down the first law code in human history, three centuries before Hammurabi, and his son Shulgi ran the most obsessively documented bureaucracy in the ancient world while also claiming, in a delightful piece of royal propaganda, to have run a 200-mile religious circuit between Nippur and Ur in a single day. The Epic of Gilgamesh was first compiled into something like its standard form in the scribal schools of this era. Then Elamite raiders sacked Ur in 2004 BCE and Sumerian quietly stopped being a living language.

title: The Old Babylonian Age — Hammurabi's Babylon
summary: For the first thirty years of his reign, Hammurabi was patient. He played his rivals against each other, repaired canals, gathered intelligence through letters with his ally Zimri-Lim of Mari, and then betrayed everyone in a roughly decade-long burst of conquests that unified Mesopotamia for the first time since Sargon. His famous law code, carved on a seven-foot pillar of black diorite now in the Louvre, was less the brutal 'eye for an eye' document of legend and more a comprehensive administrative manual for running a complex commercial society. While Hammurabi was conquering, Old Babylonian scribes were producing algebra and Pythagorean triples on clay tablets — mathematics the West would not match until the Islamic Golden Age 2,500 years later.

title: The Long Peace — Kassite Babylon
summary: In 1595 BCE a Hittite king named Mursili I marched 620 miles down the Euphrates, sacked Babylon in what may have been the longest military raid in the ancient world, and then bizarrely turned around and went home — leaving a power vacuum that the Kassites, mountain people who had been quietly drifting into Babylonian cities for generations as laborers and soldiers, simply walked into. The Kassite dynasty would rule Babylon for over 400 years, the longest dynasty in all of Babylonian history, by going completely native and patiently preserving the Mesopotamian cultural heritage. This is also the era of the Amarna Letters, an accidentally discovered archive of Bronze Age diplomatic correspondence in which Hittite, Egyptian, and Babylonian kings all write to each other in cuneiform Akkadian — the lingua franca of the entire Bronze Age Near East.

title: Assyria's First Rise
summary: Around 1225 BCE an Assyrian king named Tukulti-Ninurta I conquered Babylon for the first time and was promptly murdered by his own sons — beginning a depressingly recurring Assyrian pattern. Then the entire Bronze Age world collapsed within fifty years: the Hittites vanished, Mycenaean Greece fell into a Dark Age, and trade networks shattered under the combined pressure of drought, the mysterious Sea Peoples, and cascading system failure. Out of the wreckage came the Aramaic language (which would eventually replace Akkadian everywhere, including in the mouth of Jesus), the Babylonian creation epic Enuma Elish, and the strangely brilliant Babylonian substitute king ritual — in which a commoner was temporarily installed on the throne to absorb a predicted lunar eclipse omen on the real king's behalf.

title: The Assyrian War Machine
summary: Starting around 911 BCE, Assyria reinvented warfare as a deliberate instrument of state terror. The first great Neo-Assyrian king, Ashurnasirpal II, built a brand-new capital at Nimrud and threw an inaugural feast for 69,574 guests, washed down with documented quantities of beer and 10,000 sheep. His palace walls were lined with carved limestone reliefs showing flayings, impalements, and lion hunts in continuous narrative sequences — the comic-strip ancestor of all Western narrative art. His son Shalmaneser III campaigned in 31 of his 35 years on the throne, and on his Black Obelisk we have the only surviving contemporary portrait of any Israelite king (Jehu, kneeling in submission).

title: Assyria at its Zenith
summary: In 745 BCE Tiglath-Pileser III seized the throne, built the world's first true professional standing army, and systematized mass deportation as a tool of imperial control — over a million people would be uprooted and resettled across the empire over the next century. His successors conquered Israel (creating the Ten Lost Tribes), besieged Jerusalem (and were stopped by a probable epidemic the Bible remembered as an angel killing 185,000 men), conquered Egypt under Esarhaddon, and produced Ashurbanipal — a literate warrior-king who could read dead Sumerian and assembled the world's first systematically collected library at Nineveh. When Nineveh burned in 612 BCE, the fires accidentally baked Ashurbanipal's library into hard ceramic, preserving the Epic of Gilgamesh and most of what we know about Mesopotamian literature for archaeologists to find 2,500 years later.

title: Babylon Resurgent
summary: Nebuchadnezzar II reigned for 43 years and turned Babylon into the most magnificent city the ancient world had ever seen — walls of brilliant blue glazed brick at the Ishtar Gate, the 91-meter Etemenanki ziggurat that the Hebrew Bible would remember as the Tower of Babel, and possibly the legendary Hanging Gardens (though some scholars think those were actually at Nineveh). His Chaldean astronomers invented the 12-sign zodiac we still use today and worked out mathematical methods of predicting planetary positions that Hipparchus and Ptolemy would later build all of Greek astronomy on top of. He also destroyed Solomon's Temple in 586 BCE and deported the Judean elite to Babylon — and during the resulting Exile, Jewish scribes reinvented their religion as a portable, text-based faith, setting up everything that came after.

title: The End — Cyrus and the Long Echo
summary: In 539 BCE the gates of Babylon were opened to Cyrus the Great of Persia without a fight — the priesthood and population simply welcomed him as a liberator from Nabonidus, the eccentric scholar-king who had spent ten years sulking in an Arabian oasis instead of performing Marduk's rituals. Three thousand years of independent Mesopotamian political power ended that day, but Mesopotamian culture didn't end. It got disseminated. The 60-minute hour, the 360-degree circle, the 12 zodiac signs, the concept of written law, the narrative form of the biblical flood, and the basic template of the city itself all trace back to the land between the rivers — mostly invisible to us now because the influence is so old and so deeply embedded that it has become the air we breathe.
